question: A 72-year-old man underwent pancreatoduodenectomy for a large pancreatic head neoplasm. Final pathology reported a high tumor grade (G3) with negative lymph nodes and negative resection margins. Based on the provided CT image, which imaging finding would most strongly support a favorable long-term prognosis?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the axial CT image for signs that would alter prognosis: I look for tumor borders and any obvious extension into adjacent organs, gross vascular involvement of major vessels in this plane, focal hepatic lesions, peritoneal deposits, and visibly enlarged regional lymph nodes. On this slice I do not see focal hepatic lesions or peritoneal implants, and there is no clear encasement of major vessels within the field of view. There are no conspicuously enlarged regional lymph nodes apparent on this image, and the lesion appears relatively well demarcated from surrounding tissues at this level.

Next, I relate these observations to the question, which asks which imaging finding most strongly supports a favorable long-term prognosis after resection of a pancreatic head neoplasm. The options include a well‑circumscribed, noninvasive mass (A) versus findings that indicate spread (B–E). Findings that indicate metastatic disease or vascular encasement (options B–E) would predict a worse outlook because they reflect regional or distant spread or unresectability.

Then I consider how each option aligns with the image: option A (well‑circumscribed mass without invasion) matches the impression of a localized, sharply marginated lesion on this slice and therefore most strongly suggests a favorable prognosis. Options B–E (multiple enlarged nodes, vascular encasement, hepatic metastases, peritoneal implants) represent regional or distant spread that would worsen prognosis and are not seen here.

In summary, the image findings most consistent with a favorable long-term prognosis are a well‑circumscribed mass without invasion, which corresponds to option A.
caption: Abdominal CT scan showing a NF-PTEN located in the head of the pancreas. It is important to note that there is no evidence of invasion of adjacent tissue by the tumour
sub_caption: Abdominal CT scan showing a NF-PTEN located in the head of the pancreas. It is important to note that there is no evidence of invasion of adjacent tissue by the tumour.
image_context: A 72-year-old male was admitted to the hospital because of a tumour of the pancreas. Three months prior to the hospital admission an ultrasound scan had been performed regarding asymptomatic abdominal pain of unknown aetiology. The examination had demonstrated a tumour in the head of the pancreas about 11.5 cm in size. Besides the intermittent nonspecific abdominal pain, patient did not complain of any other disturbances. He suffered from hypertension and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) as comorbidities. The patient’s past medical history was notable for cholecystectomy and pneumonia a year previously. On admission, his general condition was good. He did not complain of any problems. According to a physical examination, a palpable mass in the epigastric region was revealed. The patient denied weight loss, elevated temperature, jaundice, or problems with bowel movements. Diagnostics were implemented. An abdominal computed tomography (CT) scan and endocrine evaluation was provided. Imaging of abdominal CT scan demonstrated a huge tumour in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen measuring 14 × 11 × 10 cm (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="PG-11-28118-g001">Figure 1</xref>). The pathological mass demonstrated features of central necrosis and calcifications. The enhancement due to CT scan was weak and non-equally distributed. Both the liver and other abdominal organs did not reveal any pathology. Bile ducts, both extra- and intrahepatic, were not dilated. Computed tomography scan revealed no evidence of invasion or infiltration of adjacent organs. The patient was qualified for surgery. During the laparotomy a tumour located in the head of the pancreas was found. It was a movable tumour with a rich pathological blood plexus in its wall, without evidence of invasion into adjacent organs. It was fully encapsulated. No lymphadenopathy was founded during the surgery. Intraoperatively a specimen of the tumour was taken, which confirmed the neuroendocrine nature of the tumour. Pancreatoduodenectomy was performed (). The pathological mass demonstrated features of central necrosis and calcifications. The enhancement due to CT scan was weak and non-equally distributed. Both the liver and other abdominal organs did not reveal any pathology. Bile ducts, both extra- and intrahepatic, were not dilated. Computed tomography scan revealed no evidence of invasion or infiltration of adjacent organs. The patient was qualified for surgery. During the laparotomy a tumour located in the head of the pancreas was found. It was a movable tumour with a rich pathological blood plexus in its wall, without evidence of invasion into adjacent organs. It was fully encapsulated. No lymphadenopathy was founded during the surgery. Intraoperatively a specimen of the tumour was taken, which confirmed the neuroendocrine nature of the tumour. Pancreatoduodenectomy was performed (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="PG-11-28118-g002">Figure 2</xref>). The histopathological report revealed a neuroendocrine tumour of the pancreas. According to WHO 2010 Classification, a neuroendocrine tumour – NET G3 was confirmed. All lymph nodes were free from neoplastic cells. Neoplastic infiltration was limited to the head of the pancreas without evidence of infiltration of peripancreatic tissue, duodenum, and stomach. The final pathology did not reveal positive resection margins of the tumour. Immunostaining with chromogranin A and synaptophysin confirmed the neuroendocrine features of the tumour (). The histopathological report revealed a neuroendocrine tumour of the pancreas. According to WHO 2010 Classification, a neuroendocrine tumour – NET G3 was confirmed. All lymph nodes were free from neoplastic cells. Neoplastic infiltration was limited to the head of the pancreas without evidence of infiltration of peripancreatic tissue, duodenum, and stomach. The final pathology did not reveal positive resection margins of the tumour. Immunostaining with chromogranin A and synaptophysin confirmed the neuroendocrine features of the tumour (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="PG-11-28118-g003">Figure 3</xref>). Based on mitotic count < 2 per 10 high-power fields and Ki67 index using MIB1 > 20% tumour grading G3 was assumed (). Based on mitotic count < 2 per 10 high-power fields and Ki67 index using MIB1 > 20% tumour grading G3 was assumed (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="PG-11-28118-g004">Figures 4</xref>––<xref ref-type="fig" rid="PG-11-28118-g006">6</xref>).).
relevant_image_context: A 72-year-old male was admitted to the hospital because of a tumour of the pancreas. Imaging of abdominal CT scan demonstrated a huge tumour in the head of the pancreas measuring 14 × 11 × 10 cm. The enhancement due to CT scan was weak and non-equally distributed. Computed tomography scan revealed no evidence of invasion or infiltration of adjacent organs. During the laparotomy a tumour located in the head of the pancreas was found. It was a movable tumour with a rich pathological blood plexus in its wall, without evidence of invasion into adjacent organs. Pancreatoduodenectomy was performed. The histopathological report revealed a neuroendocrine tumour of the pancreas. According to WHO 2010 Classification, a neuroendocrine tumour – NET G3 was confirmed. All lymph nodes were free from neoplastic cells. Neoplastic infiltration was limited to the head of the pancreas without evidence of infiltration of peripancreatic tissue, duodenum, and stomach. The final pathology did not reveal positive resection margins of the tumour. Immunostaining with chromogranin A and synaptophysin confirmed the neuroendocrine features of the tumour.
